# 2. Data

2.1 Data sources and construction
---------------------------------

We use four waves of the Ghana Living Standards Survey (GLSS): 1998/99 (GLSS4), 2005/06 (GLSS5), 2012/13 (GLSS6), and 2016/17 (GLSS7). These are repeated cross-sectional surveys that are representative at the national and regional levels. The GLSS uses a two-stage stratified sampling design. In the first stage, enumeration areas are selected as primary sampling units across Ghana's erstwhile ten regions. In the second stage, households are randomly selected within each enumeration area for detailed interviews.

Except for the resource extraction variables, all household- and farm-level variables come from a consolidated farmer-level GLSS dataset \[52\], which has been used in several previous studies \[53\], \[54\], \[55\], \[56\], \[57\], \[58\], \[59\]. We restrict the sample to crop-producing households and exclude crop yields below the 2.5th percentile or above the 97.5th percentile within each crop and survey wave to reduce the influence of extreme observations. The final sample contains 26,811 farm operators cultivating one or more of the following crops: banana, beans, cashew, cassava, citrus, cocoa, coconut, cocoyam, cotton, kenaf, maize, millet, palm, peanuts, plantain, potato, rice, sorghum, yam, sugarcane, tomato, pepper, okra, onion, eggplant, and other crops.

The consolidated dataset does not contain direct community-level measures of extractive activity. We therefore recover these indicators from the original GLSS community questionnaires and merge them into the farmer-level data using enumeration area identifiers. The community questionnaire records information on demographic characteristics, local economic activities, infrastructure, health and education facilities, agricultural practices, and development trends. In each community, interviewers collect this information from up to 20 opinion leaders, including chiefs, elders, assembly members, unit committee members, teachers, nurses, and other knowledgeable residents. The survey protocol requires these respondents to reflect the occupational, ethnic, gender, and religious diversity of the community.

We classify a community as an extraction locality if extractive activity is listed among the top four economic activities reported by community[^2] opinion leaders. For GLSS7, we also use information on the primary occupations of temporary workers visiting communities for seasonal employment. The main extraction categories used in this study are gold and bauxite mining, sand winning, and stone quarrying. Mining includes both commercial mineral mining and informal or small-scale mineral mining. Communities may report more than one extractive activity, so our aggregate extraction indicator equals one if any of these activities is present. The disaggregated indicators allow us to examine whether different extractive land uses are associated with different technology and efficiency outcomes

2.2 Descriptive statistics
--------------------------

Table 1 summarizes the main characteristics of crop producers in the pooled sample and by extraction status. The average farmer is about 47 years old, has four years of education, and lives in a household of about five adult-equivalent members. About one-quarter of producers are female, and the average dependency ratio is 1.42. The average farmer cultivates 1.84 hectares, and about 59 percent own their farmland. Mean crop output, expressed in maize-equivalent kilograms per hectare, is 1,049, although there is substantial variation across crops and communities.

Input use remains low for some productivity-enhancing technologies. Average expenditure on planting materials is about GHS 100 per hectare, while farmers use about 20 hired labour man-days, 175 kg of fertilizer, and 13 litres of pesticide per hectare. Access to irrigation, mechanization, and credit is limited, with mean access rates of about 1%, 9%, and 17%, respectively. These descriptive patterns tell of a smallholder production environment in which productivity depends strongly on land, labour, and access to modern inputs.

The disaggregated summary statistics in Table S1 show that producers in extraction communities differ from the pooled sample in several respects, although the pattern varies by extraction activity. Farmers in mining communities tend to be older and more educated than the pooled average, while female representation is lower across the main extraction categories. Commercial mining communities record relatively high average yields for several crops, but the number of observations is small. Informal or small-scale mining communities show higher average fertilizer and pesticide use than the pooled sample, while quarrying communities show lower land size and lower planting material, fertilizer, and pesticide use. Sand winning communities have relatively high land ownership and credit access, but lower average planting material use. These differences reinforce the need to avoid treating all forms of extraction as equivalent.

Table A2 in the appendix reports trends in farmer, household, production, and input characteristics across the survey waves. In the pooled sample, education, household labour, irrigation access, and several crop yields increase over time, while average land size, crop diversification, mechanization, and credit access decline. Trends also differ by extraction activity. For instance, informal or small-scale mining communities record rising education levels and increases in several crop yields, but declining crop diversification and mechanization. Quarrying communities show increases in household labour and some input-use measures, while sand winning communities show declining mechanization and credit access. These patterns suggest that observed differences between extraction and non-extraction communities may reflect both extraction exposure and broader structural changes in Ghanaian agriculture.

Overall, the descriptive statistics provide two motivations for the empirical strategy. First, extraction communities differ from non-extraction communities in observable farmer, household, input, and crop characteristics, making simple mean comparisons insufficient. Second, the differences are not uniform across mining, quarrying, and sand winning. We therefore use matching to improve comparability across communities and a meta-stochastic frontier framework to distinguish technology gaps from differences in technical efficiency.
